title: Map Your Electorate
description: Create a comprehensive profile of an Australian federal electorate — demographics, voting history, key issues, major employers, community organisations, and media landscape. This is the intelligence foundation for any campaign.
whyItMatters: You cannot win an electorate you do not understand. Every successful campaign starts with deep local knowledge — who lives there, what they care about, how they voted last time, and what has changed since. This project teaches you to build that intelligence from publicly available data.

steps.title: Choose an electorate
steps.description: Select an Australian federal electorate. Marginal seats are more interesting but any electorate works.
steps.tips: Use the AEC website to find electorate profiles and boundary maps.

steps.title: Demographic analysis
steps.description: Use ABS Census data to profile the electorate: age distribution, household income, education levels, housing tenure, country of birth, languages spoken.
steps.tips: Compare the electorate to the national average to identify what makes it distinctive.

steps.title: Voting history
steps.description: Compile the last four election results: primary votes, two-party preferred, preference flows, booth-by-booth variation.
steps.tips: Use the AEC Tally Room for historical data. Identify which booths swing and which are locked in.

steps.title: Issue mapping
steps.description: Identify the top five local issues through local media, council records, community group websites, and social media analysis.
steps.tips: Local issues (hospital closures, transport, development) often matter more than national ones.

steps.title: Stakeholder landscape
steps.description: Map the key stakeholders: major employers, community organisations, local media, sporting clubs, religious institutions, advocacy groups.
steps.tips: These are the networks that influence local opinion. A campaign must engage with them.

steps.title: Strategic assessment
steps.description: Write a one-page strategic assessment: is this seat winnable, what would it take, and what are the biggest risks?
steps.tips: Be honest about the numbers. If the margin is 15%, acknowledge the seat is not competitive this cycle.

deliverables: A 10-page electorate profile document with maps, data tables, and analysis
A stakeholder map with key contacts and influence ratings

rubric.description: Is the demographic and voting data comprehensive, current, and properly sourced?
Does the profile go beyond national data to capture genuinely local issues and dynamics?
Is the strategic assessment honest and evidence-based, not wishful thinking?
Are key local stakeholders identified with their influence and alignment assessed?
Is the document well-organised, visually clear, and usable by a campaign team?
